turns.content: I'm trying to migrate some performance-critical game logic into our new Rust codebase, and I've hit a wall on the API surface. My current JSON wrapper uses serde_json::Value for dynamic data structures, but when I try to bump the minor version to add a new field, cargo-semver-checks flags it as a major breaking change even though it feels like an additive update. Can someone explain why this is happening?

The issue stems from how semver detects breaking changes in public APIs. When you add a field to a struct or modify a method signature, cargo-semver-checks interprets that as potentially breaking existing consumers who rely on the old structure. Think of it like adding a mandatory column to an Excel sheet; if your clients were writing formulas assuming certain columns existed and others didn't, suddenly changing what is required breaks their code even if you aren't removing anything. The tool checks for any change in public visibility or signature that could affect downstream users.

But wait, I only added a field and kept all existing fields optional, so how does that break things? My clients shouldn't care if I add more data. And also, why did my #[doc(hidden)] comment on an internal helper struct still trigger the failure?

You're right that adding fields alone isn't always breaking for well-designed APIs, but cargo-semver-checks runs strict checks because in Rust, even small signature changes can break serialization logic or deserialization patterns. As for the #[doc(hidden)] comment, while it hides items from generated docs, certain operations like macro expansion or trait implementations might still expose them to external inspection depending on visibility modifiers and where they are used. The tool scans all public APIs regardless of whether they have #[doc(hidden)], so if that struct is part of a larger public type's implementation details, it gets flagged.
I keep getting false positives from my semver check runs and I suspect #[doc(hidden)] is the culprit. Why does hiding a field in docs break compatibility even if it's not public?

That is a classic trap with cargo-semver-checks. When you mark something with #[doc(hidden)], the tool still counts it as part of your public API surface because semver considers any documented item, regardless of visibility attributes like #[cfg(test)] or #[doc(hidden)]. Think of it like a secret room in your house; even if no one walks through the door to enter it, the fact that you built the walls and floor means the room exists structurally. If someone adds documentation about that hidden field tomorrow, the tool sees a new public entry point and flags it as a breaking change. The solution is usually to exclude those items from the API report entirely by using #[doc(hidden)] combined with #[cfg(feature = "internal")] or simply avoid documenting them at all so they never appear in the generated report. This prevents the false positive while keeping your internal implementation details private.
